nodes.title: Discrete Mathematics Review
nodes.content: Comprehensive review of sets, functions, proofs, sequences, summations, induction, recursion, counting, permutations, and combinations.

nodes.title: Sets, Functions, Proofs (Review)
nodes.content: Introduction to fundamental concepts in discrete mathematics including set theory, functions, and various proof techniques.

nodes.title: Sets and Subsets
nodes.content: Core definitions and properties of sets, including types of sets, cardinality, and basic set relationships.

nodes.title: Set
nodes.content: A collection of distinct objects.

nodes.title: Element
nodes.content: An individual object that is part of a set.

nodes.title: Roster Notation
nodes.content: Defining a set by listing its elements within curly braces, separated by commas (e.g., {1, 2, 3}).

nodes.title: Empty Set (∅ or {})
nodes.content: The unique set containing no elements, also known as the null set.

nodes.title: Finite Set
nodes.content: A set that is either empty or whose elements can be numbered from 1 to n for some positive integer n.

nodes.title: Infinite Set
nodes.content: A set that is not finite (its elements cannot be numbered from 1 to n).

nodes.title: Cardinality (|A|)
nodes.content: The number of distinct elements in a finite set A.

nodes.title: Natural Numbers (ℕ)
nodes.content: The set of all integers greater than or equal to 0 (0, 1, 2, ...).

nodes.title: Integers (ℤ)
nodes.content: The set of all positive and negative whole numbers and zero (..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, ...).

nodes.title: Rational Numbers (ℚ)
nodes.content: The set of real numbers that can be expressed as a/b, where a and b are integers and b ≠ 0.

nodes.title: Irrational Numbers
nodes.content: The set of real numbers that cannot be expressed as a/b, where a and b are integers and b ≠ 0 (e.g., √2, π).

nodes.title: Real Numbers (ℝ)
nodes.content: The set of all rational and irrational numbers.

nodes.title: Positive Number
nodes.content: A number x is positive if and only if x > 0.

nodes.title: Negative Number
nodes.content: A number x is negative if and only if x < 0.

nodes.title: Nonnegative Number
nodes.content: A number x is nonnegative if and only if x ≥ 0.

nodes.title: Set Builder Notation
nodes.content: Defines a set by specifying elements from a larger set that satisfy certain conditions (e.g., {x | x ∈ Z, x > 0}).

nodes.title: Universal Set (U)
nodes.content: A set that contains all possible elements relevant to a particular context.

nodes.title: Venn Diagrams
nodes.content: Pictorial representations used to show relationships between sets.

nodes.title: Subset (A ⊆ B)
nodes.content: If every element in set A is also an element of set B, then A is a subset of B.

nodes.title: Proper Subset (A ⊂ B)
nodes.content: A is a proper subset of B if A is a subset of B and A is not equal to B (meaning B contains at least one element not in A).

nodes.title: Sets of Sets
nodes.content: Discussion of sets whose elements are themselves sets.

nodes.title: Power Set (P(A))
nodes.content: The set of all possible subsets of a given set A.

nodes.title: Cardinality of a Power Set
nodes.content: If a finite set A has cardinality |A| = n, then its power set P(A) has cardinality |P(A)| = 2^n.

nodes.title: Union and Intersection
nodes.content: Fundamental set operations for combining or finding common elements between sets.

nodes.title: Set Intersection (A ∩ B)
nodes.content: The set containing all elements that are common to both set A and set B.

nodes.title: Set Union (A ∪ B)
nodes.content: The set containing all elements that are in set A, or in set B, or in both.

nodes.title: Definition of Functions
nodes.content: Formal definition of functions, their properties, and related terminology.

nodes.title: Function (f: X → Y)
nodes.content: A relation where each element in the domain X maps to exactly one element in the target Y.

nodes.title: Domain, Target, Co-domain
nodes.content: For f: X → Y, X is the domain (input set), and Y is the target or co-domain (set of possible outputs).

nodes.title: Well-Defined Function
nodes.content: A function is well-defined if it maps each element of its domain to exactly one element in its target; it does not map to zero or more than one element.

nodes.title: Arrow Diagram (Function)
nodes.content: A visual representation of a function using lists for domain and target elements, and arrows to show mappings.

nodes.title: Range of a Function
nodes.content: For function f: X → Y, the range is the set of all actual output values {y ∈ Y | ∃x ∈ X such that f(x) = y}.

nodes.title: Equal Functions
nodes.content: Two functions f and g are equal if they have the same domain, same target, and f(x) = g(x) for every element x in the domain.

nodes.title: Mathematical Definitions
nodes.content: Definitions of properties of integers and numbers, including parity, divisibility, and basic inequalities.

nodes.title: Even Integer
nodes.content: An integer x is even if there exists an integer k such that x = 2k.

nodes.title: Odd Integer
nodes.content: An integer x is odd if there exists an integer k such that x = 2k + 1.

nodes.title: Parity
nodes.content: The property of a number being either odd or even.

nodes.title: Same / Opposite Parity
nodes.content: Same parity: two numbers are both even or both odd. Opposite parity: one number is odd and the other is even.

nodes.title: Divides (x | y)
nodes.content: An integer x divides an integer y if and only if x ≠ 0 and y = kx for some integer k.

nodes.title: Multiple, Factor, Divisor
nodes.content: If x divides y, then y is a multiple of x, and x is a factor or divisor of y.

nodes.title: Prime Number
nodes.content: An integer n is prime if and only if n > 1, and its only positive integer divisors are 1 and n.

nodes.title: Composite Number
nodes.content: An integer n is composite if and only if n > 1, and there is an integer m such that 1 < m < n and m divides n (i.e., it has divisors other than 1 and itself).

nodes.title: At Least / Greater Than or Equal To (≥)
nodes.content: x ≥ c means x = c or x > c.

nodes.title: At Most / Less Than or Equal To (≤)
nodes.content: x ≤ c means x = c or x < c.

nodes.title: Nonpositive Number
nodes.content: A number x is nonpositive if and only if x ≤ 0.

nodes.title: Introduction to Proofs
nodes.content: Fundamental concepts and methods used to demonstrate the truth of mathematical statements.

nodes.title: Theorem
nodes.content: A statement that can be proven to be true.

nodes.title: Proof
nodes.content: A series of logically sound steps that follow from assumptions or previously proven statements, concluding with the theorem being proven.

nodes.title: Axioms
nodes.content: Statements that are assumed to be true without proof, serving as foundational truths for proofs.

nodes.title: Perfect Square
nodes.content: A number n is a perfect square if n = k² for some integer k.

nodes.title: Proof by Exhaustion
nodes.content: A proof method where a universal statement is proven by checking each element individually when the domain is small and finite.

nodes.title: Universal Generalization
nodes.content: A proof method for universal statements by proving it for an arbitrary (non-specific) object in the domain.

nodes.title: Consecutive Integers
nodes.content: Two integers such that one is equal to 1 plus the other (e.g., n and n+1).

nodes.title: Counterexample
nodes.content: A specific assignment of values to variables that demonstrates a universal statement is false.

nodes.title: Existence Proof
nodes.content: A proof that demonstrates an existential statement (something exists) is true.

nodes.title: Constructive Existence Proof
nodes.content: An existence proof that provides a specific example or a method to construct an element with the required properties.

nodes.title: Nonconstructive Existence Proof
nodes.content: An existence proof that proves an element with the required properties exists without explicitly giving or constructing it.

nodes.title: Best Practices and Common Errors in Proofs
nodes.content: Guidelines for writing clear and valid proofs, along with common pitfalls to avoid.

nodes.title: Existential Instantiation
nodes.content: A logical rule that allows an object, known to exist, to be given a specific name for use in a proof, provided the name is new.

nodes.title: Common Error: Generalizing from Examples
nodes.content: Mistake of assuming a statement holds true for all cases based on observations from a few specific examples.

nodes.title: Common Error: Skipping Steps
nodes.content: Omitting logical steps in a proof, which can lead to invalid conclusions or make the proof difficult to follow.

nodes.title: Common Error: Circular Reasoning
nodes.content: Assuming the conclusion of the argument as one of the premises, making the proof invalid.

nodes.title: Common Error: Assuming Unproven Facts
nodes.content: Using statements as true within a proof that have not been established as axioms or previously proven theorems.

nodes.title: Sum and Sequences
nodes.content: Exploration of ordered lists of elements (sequences) and the notation and methods for summing their terms.

nodes.title: Sequences
nodes.content: Definitions and properties of sequences, including types and ways to define them.

nodes.title: Sequence
nodes.content: A special type of function whose domain is a set of consecutive integers (e.g., {a_m, a_{m+1}, ..., a_n}).

nodes.title: Term and Index (Sequence)
nodes.content: In a sequence, a_k is a term, and k is its index.

nodes.title: Finite Sequence
nodes.content: A sequence with a finite domain.

nodes.title: Finite Sequence Properties
nodes.content: A finite sequence has an initial index m, a final index n (n ≥ m), an initial term a_m, and a final term a_n.

nodes.title: Infinite Sequence
nodes.content: A sequence with an infinite domain.

nodes.title: Explicit Formula (Sequence)
nodes.content: A formula that directly defines the value of a term a_k based on its index k, without reference to other terms.

nodes.title: Increasing Sequence
nodes.content: A sequence where for every two consecutive indices, k and k+1, a_k < a_{k+1}.

nodes.title: Nondecreasing Sequence
nodes.content: A sequence where for every two consecutive indices, k and k+1, a_k ≤ a_{k+1}.

nodes.title: Decreasing Sequence
nodes.content: A sequence where for every two consecutive indices, k and k+1, a_k > a_{k+1}.

nodes.title: Nonincreasing Sequence
nodes.content: A sequence where for every two consecutive indices, k and k+1, a_k ≥ a_{k+1}.

nodes.title: Geometric Sequence / Common Ratio
nodes.content: A sequence where each term after the initial term is found by multiplying the previous term by a fixed number called the common ratio (r).

nodes.title: Arithmetic Sequence / Common Difference
nodes.content: A sequence where each term after the initial term is found by adding a fixed number called the common difference (d) to the previous term.

nodes.title: Recurrence Relations
nodes.content: Defining sequences by relating each term to previous terms, used in dynamical systems.

nodes.title: Recurrence Relation
nodes.content: A rule that defines a term a_n as a function of previous terms in the sequence (e.g., a_n = f(a_{n-1}, a_{n-2}, ...)).

nodes.title: Fibonacci Sequence
nodes.content: A classic sequence defined by the recurrence relation F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2) with F(0)=0, F(1)=1.

nodes.title: Dynamical System
nodes.content: A system that evolves over time, where the state at any point is determined by rules dependent on past states.

nodes.title: Discrete Time Dynamical System
nodes.content: A dynamical system where time is divided into discrete intervals, and the state in one interval is a function of states in previous intervals.

nodes.title: Summations
nodes.content: Notation and methods for expressing and evaluating the sum of terms in numerical sequences.

nodes.title: Summation Notation (Σ)
nodes.content: A concise way to represent the sum of terms in a numerical sequence, specifying the index, lower limit, and upper limit.

nodes.title: Index, Lower Limit, Upper Limit (Summation)
nodes.content: In Σ_{j=s to t} a_j, j is the index, s is the lower limit, and t is the upper limit of the summation.

nodes.title: Summation Form / Expanded Form
nodes.content: Summation form is the Σ expression; expanded form is the sum written out term-by-term (e.g., 1³ + 2³ + 3³ + 4³).

nodes.title: Closed Form (Summation)
nodes.content: A mathematical expression that calculates the value of a sum without using summation notation.

nodes.title: Closed Form for Arithmetic Sequence Sum
nodes.content: A formula to find the sum of terms in an arithmetic sequence without explicitly adding each term.

nodes.title: Closed Form for Geometric Sequence Sum
nodes.content: A formula to find the sum of terms in a geometric sequence without explicitly adding each term.

nodes.title: Induction
nodes.content: A powerful set of proof techniques, including mathematical, strong, and structural induction, essential for proving statements about integers and recursively defined structures.

nodes.title: Mathematical Induction
nodes.content: The principle of mathematical induction, used to prove statements about all natural numbers.

nodes.title: Base Case (Mathematical Induction)
nodes.content: The first step in an inductive proof, establishing that the theorem is true for the initial value (e.g., n=1 or n=a).

nodes.title: Inductive Step (Mathematical Induction)
nodes.content: The step that proves if the theorem holds for an arbitrary integer k (the inductive hypothesis), then it also holds for k+1.

nodes.title: Principle of Mathematical Induction
nodes.content: If the base case is true and the inductive step is true, then the theorem holds for all integers greater than or equal to the base case.

nodes.title: Inductive Hypothesis
nodes.content: The assumption made in the inductive step that the statement S(k) is true for an arbitrary integer k.

nodes.title: More Inductive Proofs
nodes.content: Applications of mathematical induction to prove properties like divisibility, explicit formulas, and generalized laws.

nodes.title: Divisibility Proofs by Induction
nodes.content: Using mathematical induction to prove statements about integer divisibility.

nodes.title: Proving Recurrence Relations by Induction
nodes.content: Using induction to prove that an explicit formula correctly defines a sequence given by a recurrence relation.

nodes.title: Inductive Proofs for Sums
nodes.content: Using induction to prove the correctness of closed-form expressions for sums, such as arithmetic or geometric series.

nodes.title: Generalized De Morgan's Law Proof
nodes.content: Using induction to prove De Morgan's Law for an arbitrary number of sets (generalizing |A ∪ B|' and |A ∩ B|').

nodes.title: Strong Induction and Well-Ordering
nodes.content: Advanced inductive techniques and foundational principles related to the structure of integers.

nodes.title: Principle of Strong Induction
nodes.content: A variant of induction where the inductive hypothesis assumes the statement S(j) is true for all integers j from the base case up to k, to prove S(k+1).

nodes.title: Base Case (Strong Induction)
nodes.content: Establishes that S(n) holds for a range of initial values, n = a through b, rather than just a single initial value.

nodes.title: Inductive Step (Strong Induction)
nodes.content: Assumes S(j) is true for all values of j in the range from 'a' through k (where k ≥ b) and then proves that S(k+1) holds.

nodes.title: Well-Ordering Principle
nodes.content: States that any nonempty subset of the nonnegative integers must have a smallest element.

nodes.title: Well-Ordering Implies Mathematical Induction
nodes.content: The well-ordering principle can be used as a foundation to prove the validity of the principle of mathematical induction.

nodes.title: Prime Factorization Proof (Strong Induction)
nodes.content: Using strong induction to prove that every integer greater than 1 can be uniquely expressed as a product of prime numbers.

nodes.title: Division Algorithm Proof (Well-Ordering)
nodes.content: Proof of the Division Algorithm (for integers a and d, with d>0, there exist unique integers q and r such that a = dq + r and 0 ≤ r < d) using the well-ordering principle.

nodes.title: Structural Induction
nodes.content: An induction technique tailored for proving properties of recursively defined sets and structures.

nodes.title: Structural Induction (Proof Technique)
nodes.content: A proof technique used for theorems about recursively defined sets, where the proof structure mirrors the recursive definition.

nodes.title: Balanced Parentheses String
nodes.content: A string of parentheses is balanced if the number of left parentheses equals the number of right parentheses, and for any prefix, the number of left parentheses is greater than or equal to the number of right parentheses.

nodes.title: Recursion
nodes.content: Study of recursive definitions for functions and sets, and the design and analysis of recursive algorithms.

nodes.title: Recursive Definitions
nodes.content: Defining functions, sets, and structures in terms of themselves or smaller instances.

nodes.title: Factorial Function (n!)
nodes.content: Defined recursively as 0! = 1 and n! = n * (n-1)! for n > 0. A common example of a recursively defined function.

nodes.title: Recursive Definition (Concept)
nodes.content: A method of defining a function, set, or structure where the definition refers to itself on smaller inputs or simpler elements.

nodes.title: Recursion (Process)
nodes.content: The process of solving a problem or computing a value by breaking it down into smaller, similar subproblems or values and combining their solutions.

nodes.title: Basis (Recursive Definition)
nodes.content: The non-recursive part of a recursive definition that explicitly states initial elements in a set or base case values for a function.

nodes.title: Recursive Rule
nodes.content: The part of a recursive definition that shows how to construct additional elements in a set or compute values from elements/values already known to be in the set/computed.

nodes.title: Exclusion Statement (Recursive Definition)
nodes.content: A statement often included in recursive set definitions, stating that an element is in the set ONLY if it is given in the basis or can be constructed by applying the recursive rules.

nodes.title: Empty String (Λ)
nodes.content: A unique string with a length of 0.

nodes.title: Vertices and Edges (Trees)
nodes.content: A tree is a graph with vertices (nodes) and edges (connections between vertices).

nodes.title: Root (Perfect Binary Tree)
nodes.content: The designated starting vertex in a tree structure, from which all other nodes descend.

nodes.title: Recursive Definition: Properly Nested Parentheses
nodes.content: A set of rules to recursively define strings of parentheses that are correctly matched and ordered.

nodes.title: Recursive Definition: Binary Strings
nodes.content: Rules for recursively generating all possible strings composed of binary digits (0s and 1s).

nodes.title: Recursive Definition: Perfect Binary Trees
nodes.content: Rules for recursively constructing perfect binary trees from smaller perfect binary trees or a single root node.

nodes.title: Induction and Recursive Algorithms
nodes.content: Applying inductive proof techniques to formally verify the correctness of recursive algorithms.

nodes.title: Proof of Correctness (Recursive Algorithms)
nodes.content: Using mathematical induction to formally demonstrate that a recursive algorithm always produces the correct output for all valid inputs.

nodes.title: Proof Example: ReverseString Algorithm
nodes.content: An illustrative proof of correctness for a recursive algorithm that reverses the order of characters in a string.

nodes.title: Proof Example: PowerSet Algorithm
nodes.content: An illustrative proof of correctness for a recursive algorithm that computes the power set of a given set.

nodes.title: Proof Example: FastExpRec Algorithm
nodes.content: An illustrative proof of correctness for a recursive algorithm designed for efficient exponentiation.

nodes.title: Recursive Algorithms
nodes.content: Design and implementation of algorithms that solve problems by calling themselves with simpler subproblems.

nodes.title: Recursive Algorithm
nodes.content: An algorithm that solves a problem by calling itself one or more times with smaller inputs of the same problem until a base case is reached.

nodes.title: Recursive Calls
nodes.content: The instances where a recursive algorithm invokes itself during its execution.

nodes.title: Pseudo-code Comments
nodes.content: Explanatory notes in an algorithm's pseudo-code, denoted by '//', which are not part of the executable logic.

nodes.title: Factorial Recursive Algorithm
nodes.content: An algorithm implementing the recursive definition of the factorial function.

nodes.title: Power Set Recursive Algorithm
nodes.content: An algorithm that computes the power set of a given set using a recursive approach.

nodes.title: Reverse String Recursive Algorithm
nodes.content: An algorithm that reverses a string by recursively processing substrings.

nodes.title: Fibonacci Recursive Algorithm
nodes.content: A direct implementation of the Fibonacci recurrence relation, which can be computationally inefficient for large n.

nodes.title: Nonrecursive Fibonacci Algorithm
nodes.content: An iterative algorithm to compute Fibonacci numbers, typically more efficient than its naive recursive counterpart by avoiding redundant calculations.

nodes.title: Analyzing Recursive Algorithms
nodes.content: Methods for evaluating the efficiency, particularly time complexity, of recursive algorithms.

nodes.title: Time Complexity (T(n))
nodes.content: A function that measures the number of elementary operations an algorithm performs as a function of its input size n.

nodes.title: Deriving Recurrence Relation for Time Complexity
nodes.content: The process of formulating a recurrence relation that describes the time complexity of a recursive algorithm.

nodes.title: Asymptotic Analysis of Recursive Algorithms
nodes.content: Techniques (like solving recurrence relations or using the Master Theorem) to determine the long-term growth rate of an algorithm's time complexity.

nodes.title: Counting Basics
nodes.content: Fundamental principles and techniques for determining the number of possible outcomes or arrangements.

nodes.title: The Bijection Rule
nodes.content: Counting techniques based on one-to-one correspondences between sets.

nodes.title: Bijection Rule (Principle)
nodes.content: If a bijection exists between two finite sets, then they have the same cardinality (number of elements).

nodes.title: Bijection (Bijective Function)
nodes.content: A function that is both injective (one-to-one) and surjective (onto), meaning every element in the domain maps to a unique element in the target, and every element in the target has exactly one element from the domain mapped to it.

nodes.title: Inverse Function (f⁻¹)
nodes.content: For a bijection f: S → T, its inverse f⁻¹: T → S maps each element in T back to its unique corresponding element in S.

nodes.title: k-to-1 Correspondence
nodes.content: A function f: X → Y where for every y ∈ Y, there are exactly k different x ∈ X such that f(x) = y.

nodes.title: k-to-1 Rule
nodes.content: If a k-to-1 correspondence exists from set X to set Y, then the cardinality of Y is the cardinality of X divided by k (|Y| = |X| / k).

nodes.title: Sum and Product Rules
nodes.content: Basic combinatorial rules for counting outcomes of sequential or alternative events.

nodes.title: Product Rule (Counting)
nodes.content: If a procedure consists of a sequence of m tasks, and there are n_1 ways for the first task, n_2 for the second, ..., n_m for the m-th, then there are n_1 * n_2 * ... * n_m total ways.

nodes.title: Alphabet (Characters)
nodes.content: A finite set of characters from which strings are formed.

nodes.title: Strings of Length n (Σⁿ)
nodes.content: The set of all strings of a fixed length n, where each character is chosen from a given alphabet Σ.

nodes.title: Sum Rule (Counting)
nodes.content: If a task can be performed in either n_1 ways or n_2 ways, and these two sets of ways are mutually exclusive, then there are n_1 + n_2 total ways to perform the task.

nodes.title: The Generalized Product Rule
nodes.content: An extension of the product rule for counting sequences of choices.

nodes.title: Generalized Product Rule (Principle)
nodes.content: If selecting an item involves a sequence of steps, and the number of choices at each step is independent of previous choices, the total number of items is the product of the number of choices at each step.

nodes.title: The Pigeonhole Principle
nodes.content: A combinatorial principle stating that if items are distributed into containers, at least one container must hold more than a certain number of items.

nodes.title: Pigeonhole Principle (Basic)
nodes.content: If k+1 or more pigeons are placed into k pigeonholes, then at least one pigeonhole must contain more than one pigeon.

nodes.title: Generalized Pigeonhole Principle
nodes.content: If N objects are placed into k boxes, then at least one box must contain at least ⌈N/k⌉ objects.

nodes.title: Converse of Generalized Pigeonhole Principle
nodes.content: A related concept exploring scenarios where a certain outcome is *not* guaranteed, or determining minimum conditions to *avoid* a specific outcome of the pigeonhole principle.

nodes.title: Inclusion-Exclusion Principle
nodes.content: A counting technique for finding the cardinality of the union of multiple sets by accounting for overlaps.

nodes.title: Principle of Inclusion-Exclusion (PIE)
nodes.content: A technique to determine the cardinality of the union of sets; for two sets, |A ∪ B| = |A| + |B| - |A ∩ B|.

nodes.title: Mutually Disjoint Sets
nodes.content: A collection of sets where the intersection of every pair of sets in the collection is empty (they share no common elements).

nodes.title: General Inclusion-Exclusion Principle
nodes.content: Extends PIE to an arbitrary number of sets, alternating sums and differences of cardinalities of intersections of increasing numbers of sets.

nodes.title: Counting by Complement
nodes.content: A counting strategy that calculates the number of elements with a property by subtracting the number of elements without that property from the total.

nodes.title: Counting by Complement (Method)
nodes.content: To count elements in a set S that have a certain property, find the total number of elements in S and subtract the number of elements in S that do not have the property.

nodes.title: Permutations and Subsets
nodes.content: Counting distinct ordered arrangements (permutations) and unordered selections (subsets/combinations) of items.

nodes.title: Counting Permutations
nodes.content: Techniques for counting arrangements where the order of items matters.

nodes.title: r-permutation (P(n,r))
nodes.content: An ordered sequence of r items chosen without repetition from a set of n distinct items. Calculated as P(n,r) = n! / (n-r)!.

nodes.title: Permutation (P(n,n) or n!)
nodes.content: An ordered arrangement of all n elements of a finite set, where each element appears exactly once. Calculated as n!.

nodes.title: Counting Subsets
nodes.content: Techniques for counting unordered selections of items.

nodes.title: r-subset
nodes.content: An unordered selection of r items from a larger set. Also known as an r-combination.

nodes.title: r-combination (C(n,r) or nCr)
nodes.content: An unordered selection of r items from a set of n distinct items without repetition. Calculated as C(n,r) = n! / (r!(n-r)!). Also known as 'n choose r'.

nodes.title: Identity (Equation)
nodes.content: An equation that is true for all values of its variables for which both sides are defined.

nodes.title: 'n choose r' Notation
nodes.content: Notation (nCr or (n k)) used to represent the number of r-combinations from a set of n elements.

nodes.title: Subset and Permutation Examples
nodes.content: Practical examples illustrating the distinction and application of permutation and combination counting methods.

nodes.title: Distinguishing Subsets vs. Permutations
nodes.content: Understanding when to use permutations (order matters) versus combinations (order does not matter) based on the problem context.

nodes.title: Permutations with Repetitions
nodes.content: Counting distinct arrangements when some items in the set are identical.

nodes.title: Permutation with Repetition (Formula)
nodes.content: The number of distinct permutations of n items, where there are n_1 identical items of type 1, n_2 of type 2, ..., n_k of type k, is n! / (n_1! n_2! ... n_k!).

nodes.title: Combinatorial Generation
nodes.content: Algorithms and methods for systematically listing all permutations and combinations of a set.

nodes.title: Generating Permutations and Combinations
nodes.content: Algorithms to produce sequences or subsets in a structured order, typically lexicographic.

nodes.title: Lexicographic Order
nodes.content: A method of ordering sequences (like n-tuples, strings, or subsets) by comparing them character by character from left to right, similar to dictionary order.

nodes.title: Permutation (n-tuple in Lexicographic Order)
nodes.content: An ordered n-tuple where each number in {1, 2, ..., n} appears exactly once, often generated according to lexicographic order.

nodes.title: Algorithm: Generate Lexicographic Permutations
nodes.content: An algorithm (e.g., Heap's algorithm or Narayana Pandita's algorithm) to systematically generate all permutations of a set in lexicographic order.

nodes.title: Algorithm: Generate Lexicographic r-subsets
nodes.content: An algorithm to systematically generate all r-subsets (combinations) of a set in lexicographic order.
